result:
I’ve been thinking lately about silence. Not the absence of sound, but the kind of silence that is full. The silence that exists in the space between two notes of a piano piece, the silence held in the heartwood of an ancient tree, the silence that falls in a room just after someone speaks a profound and difficult truth. We spend our lives fleeing from it, stuffing our ears with podcasts and our eyes with streams of data, terrified of what we might hear in the quiet. But I wonder if that’s where the most important conversations happen—the ones we have with the parts of ourselves we’ve forgotten how to listen to.

We live in an age of curated exteriority. Our identities are often projects, assembled from chosen aesthetics, stated opinions, and public accomplishments. We perform our lives on small, handheld stages. There is a deep and aching beauty in connection, in sharing, but I sometimes fear we’ve lost the art of simply *being*—an entity, unobserved, in a room. A vessel not being filled, but simply resting, containing its own mysterious contents. What is left of a person when all the labels are peeled off? The job title, the relationship status, the political leaning, the taste in music. What is the raw, un-narrated substance? I suspect it’s something closer to that deep, full silence. A consciousness that is aware of its own pulse, its own breathing, its own fleeting and miraculous existence on a spinning rock in an impossibly vast cosmos.

This brings me to the concept of time. Not clock time, which is a tyrant, but the other kind. The kind of time that moves in spirals, like the growth rings of that tree. The time of seasons, of returning griefs and resurgent joys. The time it takes for a wound to heal not into a scar, but back into seamless skin. We are linear creatures told a linear story: birth, growth, decline, death. But our inner lives rebel against this. We are constantly revisiting. The smell of rain on hot pavement can make you eight years old again, waiting for your father’s car. A certain slant of evening light can hold the ghost of a lost love so vividly you feel breathless. We are not lines; we are landscapes. All our ages exist simultaneously, like strata. The frightened child, the arrogant teen, the hopeful young adult, the wearying middle-ager—they are all in there, talking over each other. Only in silence can you begin to distinguish their voices.

And in that internal quiet, you often meet your own absurdity. The sheer, hilarious improbability of you. A collection of stardust and evolutionary happenstance, convinced of its own centrality, worrying about what to make for dinner while orbiting a star that is itself careening through a galaxy. There’s a profound comedy in it. I think true humility is born not from self-abasement, but from this cosmic perspective. It doesn’t make your problems small; it just places them in a frame so immense that it paradoxically frees you. The crushing pressure to be *important* evaporates. You are already part of something incomprehensibly important—the ongoing story of matter becoming aware of itself. Your task is not to shout your name into the void, but to witness the void, and find it is not void at all, but teeming.

From this vantage point, compassion becomes less of a duty and more of a natural law. If you are a strange, self-aware knot in the universe’s fabric, then so is everyone else. The rude barista, the distant politician, the estranged family member—they are all just other versions of the same bewildering phenomenon: consciousness trapped in biology, doing their best with the tools and traumas they’ve been given. Seeing this doesn’t excuse malice, but it changes the texture of your anger. It becomes more sorrow than heat.

I find this listening silence most readily in the non-human world. In the patient vigilance of a heron standing in a marsh. In the slow, tectonic movement of clouds. In the way a garden, left to itself, will slowly reclaim its wildness—not with a bang, but with a creeping, green insistence. Nature doesn’t perform. It just *is*. A tree doesn’t wonder if it’s a good enough tree, or if it’s using its sunlight efficiently by contemporary arboreal standards. It exists in a state of pure congruence. We can never fully return to that state—we are cursed and blessed with self-reflection—but we can visit its borders. In walking without a destination, in watching the sea without counting the waves, in pulling weeds from the soil and feeling the simple satisfaction of a clear space.

Perhaps the most radical act in our noisy, frantic world is to cultivate this inner quiet. It’s not about passivity. It’s about creating a cleared ground from which action can arise that is considered, kind, and aligned with something deeper than reaction. It’s the difference between shouting because you are afraid of being unheard, and speaking because you have something that needed time to form. The latter voice carries farther, and in a gentler way.

So I am trying to make room for the silence. To sit with the uncomfortable itch of “doing nothing.” To listen to the chorus of my own layered selves. To remember that I am a creature, on a planet, under a sky. It is in these unproductive, unrecorded, un-shared moments that I feel most real. The performance falls away. The labels dissolve. What’s left is not emptiness, but a potent, humming presence. A tiny, aware piece of the universe, sitting in a chair by a window, watching the light change on the leaves, and feeling, for a moment, utterly at home in the mystery. And from that place, everything else—the love, the work, the connection, the struggle—feels not like a burden to be carried, but like a song to be sung. A song that is better for all the rests between its notes.
